California
Fair POlitical
Practices Commission
March 1, 199 0
Louis B. Green
city Attorney,
P. O. Box 3707
Sunnyvale, CA
City of Sunnyval.e
9 4046-37 07
Re: Your Reguest for Advice
our File No. A-90-075
Dear Mr. Green:
This is j,n response to your letter requestj.ng advice on
behalf of ur. van Freidin, a member of the Sunnyvale Planning Con-
mission, regarding application of the conflict-of-interest 
provi-
sions oi tn6 poriticlt 
neforn Act (the [asgn1.1 This is a folIow-
up letter to previous advice. (Green Advice Letter No. A-89-214. )
OUESTION
ltay lrtr. Freidin participate in planning couuission decisions
relating to the downtown project area developnent?
CONCLUSION
Mr. Freidin nay participate in decisions relating to sub
areas 1, 2, and 3 of the project, including decisions requiring
amendnent of the redevelopDent plan to conform land uses in these
sub areas to the uses specified in the specific plan. These deci-
sions will not affect l,!r. Freidin in a nanner which is
distinguishable frou the effect on the public generally. 
He may
not participate in decisions relating to sub area 4, including
amendments to the redevelopment p1an, because these decisions wiLl
naterially affect his property interest and the effect is
distinguishable fron the effect on the public generally.
'I- Governnent Code Sections 81000-91015. Al1 statutory references
are to the GovernDent Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations Section
18000, et se(I. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 80? . Sacramenro CA 95804{g07 o (916) 3 22-5660


Our File No. A-9o-075
Paqe 2
FACTS
The city of Sunnlrvale is currently engaged in a planning
process for the downtown project area. Mr. Freidin is a city
planning commissioner whose residence is located within the
redevelopment project area, but just outside the boundaries of the
downtown developnent plan project area. We previously advised
that ur. Freidin night be precluded frorn participating in sorne
aspects of the plan, because the decisions nigrht have a naterial
financial effect upon his property, distingruishable from their
effect on the public generally. (Green Advice Letter, supra. )
You have retained an independent appraisal research company
to provide economic data regarding the impact of various decisions
upon ur. Freidin's property, in order to deterrnine if particular
decisions would materially affect !.rr. Friedin's property. Based
upon the information provided, you wish to know whether
liIr. Freidin uray participate in decisions involving the downtown
proj ect .
ANALYSIS
We previously advised that ltr. Freidin's inability to
participate in one part of the downtolrn plan would not necessarily
foreclose his participation in other parts of the p1an. Thus, if
the project could be segTnented and each area could be decided upon
independently, Mr. Freidin night be able to participate in deci-
sions involvinq some seg'nents of the plan even though he night
have to disqualify hinself with respect to other segments.
(Green, SIIEEII r p. 5. )
The inforrnation that you have provided indicates that the
project can be divided into four sub areas. These areas are not
interdependent and each could proceed without the others. Those
areas are as follows:
1. North of Washington Area.
2. llathilda Avenue Corri.dor.
3. Town Center lla11.
4. High-Density Residential.
Materialitv as to sub Areas 1, 2, and 3.
The North of washington Area, the !,fathilda Avenue Corridor,
and the Torrn Center UaIl sub areas are aII more than 3OO feet from
Mr. Freidin, s. property, but within 2,500 feet of his property.
Mr. freidin will be able to participate in decisions iegiriling
those sub areas unless the proposed plan will naterially affeCt
his real . property interests. (section 97103(b).) The Lest for
nateriality in this instance is whether the decisions will have a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of glorOOO or nore on the


Our File No. A-90-075
Page 3
fair market value of Mr. Freidin's residence. (Regulation
7a702.3 (a) (3) r copy enclosed. )
ur. Freidin is required to make a reasonable, good faith ef-
fort to deterrnine the financial effect of the dolrntown plan deci-
sj.ons on the fair market value of his residence. Any such
deternination nust include consideration of the following factors:
(1) The proxinity of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the magnitude of
the proposed project or change in use in relation-
ship to the property in which the official has an
interest;
(2) whether it is reasonable foreseeable that
the decision will affect the development potential
or income producing potential of the propertyi
(3) In addition to the foregoing, in the case
of residential property, whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will result in a
change to the character of the neighborhood includ-
ing, but not LiBited to, effect on traffic, view,
privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emi.s-
sions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.
Regiulation ]-8702.3 (d) (1) - (3) .
l,[r. Freidin's use of an independent appraiser to assess the
inpact of the developnent on the fair market value of his
residence constitutes a reasonable, good faith effort to uake the
deteraination of materiality, provided that the assessment
included consideration of the above factors. The report does not
specifi.cally state that these factors were considered. Horrever,
an overall reading of the appraisal report suggests that all or
nost of the factors were considered.z
!fr. Freidin should confinn that all of the factors set forth
in Regrulation 18702.3(d) were in fact taken into consideration in
assessing the effect of the project on the fair market vaLue of
his residence. If so, he is entitled to rely upon the reportrs
conclusion that the decisions relating to sub areas 1, 2 and 3
will not increase or decrease the fair narket value of his
residence by S10,000 or nore and thus will not have a naterial
financial effect.
2 Th" report does not appear
consideration of the inpact of
privacy, noise leveIs and air
the draft environmental inpact
as part of the appraisal.
to specifically refer to
the plan on Mr. Freidinrs view,
enissions, although it is noted that
report for the project was reviewed


Our File No. A-90-075
Page 4
Materialitv as to sub Area 4.
Sub area 4 is to be redeveloped as a high-density residential
neighborhood, with cornnercial developraent at ground floor 1eveI.
Building heights of 3 to 4 stories are proposed with a
transitional area of 2-story buildings. The southern boundary of
sub area o is within 300 feet of Mr. Freidin's residence.
Where the real property in which an officiaL has an interest
is located within 3oo feet of the boundaries or proposed
boundaries of the property which is the subject of the decision,
the effect of the decision is naterial, unless it will have re
financial effect on the official's property. (Regulation
l-8702.3 (a) (1).) The appraisal report indicates that the develop-
ment of sub area 4 lriIl have a negative financial impact upon l{r.
Freidin's property va1ue. Therefore, the decisions relating to
sub area 4 lrill rnaterially affect Mr. Freidin's financial inter-
est.
Materialitv of Anendments of the Redevelopnent PIan
In addition to adoption of a specific plan and various zone
changes, it is possible that the downtown project may require
aroendments to the existing redevelopment p1an. Mr. Freidin's
residence is located within the boundaries of the redevelopment
area. we have previously advised you that aDendment of the
redevelopment plan is deerned to naterially effect property within
the redevelopment area, unless it can be shown that the anendment
will have no financial effect. 
(Green Advice Letter, supra
pp. 5-6.
Public Generallv
Itr. Freidin uray stiIl be able to participate in the decisions
which uaterially affect his property if the effect on his property
is not distingruishable fron the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103.) Regulation 18703 (copy enclosed) provides, in
part:
A naterial financial effect of a governnental
decision on an official's interests, as described
in Governraent code section 87103, is distinguish-
able fron its effect on the public generally unless
the decision will affect the officialrs interest in
substantially the sane nanner as it will affect all
Dembers of the public or a sionificant secrment of
the oublic.
- The npublictr is all the persons residing, owning property, or
doing business in the jurisdiction of the agency in question. (In
re Lesan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1, copy enclosed.) In the case of the
city planning co'nnission, this would be the City of Sunn)rvale.
Consequently, for the public Aenerally exception to app1y, any
decision which wiLL naterialLy affect Mr. Freidin lrould have Lo


our File No. A-90-075
Page 5
affect a significant segrment of the city of sunnyvale in
substantially the same manner. (Dowd Advice Letter, No. A-88-214;
Burnham Advice Letter, No. A-86-210, copies enclosed.)
The coEDission has never adopted a strj,ct arithnetic test for
detenoining what constitutes a significant segrnent of the public.
However, in order to apply the public Alenerally exception, the
population affected must be large in number and heterogeneous in
nature. (In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC ops 62; Flvnn Advice Letter,
No. I-88-430, copies encfosed. )
we have recognized that residential property owners nay
constitute a significant segrent of the public. (In re olren
(19761 2 FPPC ops. 77, copy enclosed.) Although you have not
provided any statistics as to the nurnber of residential properties
in Sunnyvale, we assune that it represents a significant segrment
of the city. 
Therefore, if residential owners, as a group, will
be affected by the downtovrn plan in a nanner substantially siurilar
to l,[r. Freidin, the effect on Mr. Freidin's property will not be
considered distinguishable from the effect on the public gener-
a11y.
According to the appraisal report, the current do\rntown Plan
is related to an earlier najor redevelopnent of the downtown area.
The report indicates that a neasurernent of the effects of the
previous developnent on residential properties should be
indicative of the effect of the present develoPnent project-
Three areas were measured to examine the effect of the prior
developnent on Darket values: (1) the base area including nost of
the residential areas of sunnyvalei Ql a control area located
away from the dolrntown project area; and (3) the subject area
conLaining Mr. Freidin's residence and al'I of the residential
neighborhoods borderinq the downtot n area on the south and east-
The report concludes that location adjacent to the dolrntown
area has not affected the Darket val'ue of single fanily
residences, and that further developnent of the downtown area is
not expected to affect adjacent areas in a manner distinguishable
fron other residential neighborhoods. Holrever, the appraisal
report does indicate that the development of sub area 4 may affect
Ur. freidin's residence in a nanner which is distingruishable fron
the public generally. This is due to the close proxinity of
Mr. Freidin's residence to the high density develoPment area.
Upon revi.ew of the appraisal report, we concur that there is
nothing to indicate that the development of sub areas I, 2, and 3
will affect Mr. Freidin's property in a nanner distingruishable
from the public generally. Therefore, he nay participate in the
decisions relating to those areas. However, we believe that deci-
sions relating to sub area 4 will affect his property in a nanner
distinguishable fron the effect on the public generally.


Our File No. A-90-075
Page 6
You have not specified the precise amendments to the
redevelopment plan which will be considered by the planning com-
mission. Your previous letter indicated that the amendments would
priroarily involve amendnents to make land uses pernitted in the
development plan consistent with those specified in the proposed
precise plan. Based upon the prior discussion, we believe that
the public Aenerally exception would apply to these amendments
unLess they relate to sub area 4 development.
As previously indicated, the information that you provided
states that the decisions on the various sub areas are not inter-
related. 
When the issues come before the planning conmission, the
decisions fron which Mr. Freidin is disqualified should be
segregated and decided first. 
(Kilian Advice Letter,
No. A-89-522. )
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please contact ne at (9f6) 322-5901.
S incerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel
, 
l' 
',t-
By: Uargaret W. EIIison
counsel, Legal DJ.vision

Enclosures
